summary: Ronacher argues that prolonged social distancing is unsustainable and economically damaging, so societies need a more targeted way to suppress outbreaks. He proposes app-assisted contact tracing as the most efficient alternative: quickly identify cases, test them, and notify close contacts. He acknowledges privacy fears but outlines a decentralized design that minimizes surveillance by keeping data on devices and limiting retention. He contends that such systems are less invasive than the data collection people already accept from major tech platforms. He concludes that well-designed tracing apps or tokens are the best path to restoring normal life without sacrificing civil liberties.
key_points: Flattening the curve requires long-term restrictions that carry heavy social and economic costs.
Effective pandemic control relies on isolating cases and rapidly finding close contacts.
Contact tracing can be built with decentralized, privacy-preserving architecture and short data retention.
Public aversion to tracing apps often overlooks the extent of existing, accepted data collection by tech companies.
App or token-based tracing offers a realistic route back to normalcy with fewer civil-liberty tradeoffs.
money_quotes: The curve really only stays flat if we're doing this for a very, very long time.
We can get away without social distancing with the help of technology.
The most efficient way to fight the outbreak of a pandemic is isolating cases.
It turns out we could largely achieve what we want without a centralized infrastructure.
People are very good at misjudging data privacy.
Contact tracing if done well is significantly less privacy infringing than what many tech companies already do where we're okay with.
I also believe that contact tracing via apps or hardware tokens is our best chance to return to a largely normal life without giving up all our civil liberties.
key_insight: Privacy-preserving, decentralized contact tracing is a practical and less invasive alternative to prolonged lockdowns, and it deserves a fair, informed technical debate.
